In Exodus 9, God reveals something that should unsettle us: He possesses absolute power over creation, and He exercises it without hesitation to accomplish His purposes. The plague of livestock strikes with surgical precision—Egyptian animals die while Israelite animals live untouched. The boils afflict Egyptian magicians so severely they cannot stand. The hail destroys Egypt's crops with violent totality while Goshen remains dry. These are not random natural disasters but demonstrations of sovereign authority. God is not negotiating with Pharaoh as equals; He is displaying His dominion over every created thing. What's more, He does this openly, giving advance warning and even allowing some of Pharaoh's officials to choose whether they will believe and protect their servants and livestock. The God of the Bible is not the distant, aloof deity some imagine. He acts decisively in history, and He makes His power known.

Yet the most chilling aspect of this chapter is Pharaoh's response—or rather, his refusal to respond. After the livestock plague, "Pharaoh's heart was hardened, and he would not let the people go." After the boils, the text states plainly: "The LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart." After the hail, Pharaoh confesses "This time I have sinned" and promises to let Israel go—only to "sin again and harden his heart" the moment relief comes. This is the devastating pattern of Exodus 9: God's mercy and judgment are both rejected. Pharaoh witnesses undeniable proof of God's existence and power yet chooses to resist. Some scholars wrestle with the language of God "hardening" Pharaoh's heart, and Scripture itself shows Pharaoh hardening his own heart, but the truth is relentless: persistent rejection of God's authority leads to deeper obstinacy. Each refusal makes the next one easier.

What makes Pharaoh's rebellion so stark is the clarity of what God demands and offers. The Lord tells Moses in verse 16 the reason for this entire sequence: "I might display My power to you, and that My name might be proclaimed in all the earth." God is not hidden or obscure here. He gives Pharaoh multiple opportunities, clear warnings, and tangible evidence. He even shows mercy by sparing the land of Goshen and by giving Pharaoh's officials time to shelter their households. Yet Pharaoh chooses pride and power over obedience. And the consequences ripple outward—the hail kills servants and animals of those who did not believe God's warning, not just those who refused.

This chapter confronts us with a question we cannot avoid: What will we do when God speaks clearly? Pharaoh had no excuse. He saw the plagues, heard the message, witnessed the distinction between Egypt and Goshen. Yet he chose to harden his heart. We may not face hail from heaven, but we face choices about whether we will submit to God's word when it challenges our will, our comfort, our status. The God of Exodus 9 is not a God we can acknowledge casually or on our own terms. He demands worship, obedience, and allegiance. The question is whether we, having seen His power displayed throughout Scripture and even in our own lives, will respond with the submission that Pharaoh refused—or whether we will join him in the path of hardening ourselves against the one God who alone deserves our worship.
